fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that auth strategy wrapper we've been talking about. Basically, devs are tired of hand-rolling all the boilerplate every time they wire up a new identity provider — they keep getting the URLs wrong, forgetting to validate tokens properly, and the user profile shapes coming back are inconsistent depending on which flow they used. It's causing a bunch of support tickets.

The library should be configurable once with the provider details and then just handle everything: figuring out the right endpoints automatically (but still letting you override them if needed), building the login redirect params based on what hints are available, doing the full token checking sequence, and spitting out a consistent user object regardless of whether it came from the legacy format or the newer OIDC style.

For the token checks, refer to the same claim-by-claim validation ordering we settled on during that login module refactor — same priority sequence, same error categories. The profile normalization should handle the 'pipe-delimited ID' edge cases the same way we discussed.

One thing that burned us before: the config setup must not touch the caller's original options object. That caused a really subtle bug last time in staging.

Please separate concerns properly — don't dump everything in one file. Each major responsibility should live in its own module. The adapter layer that handles the JSON in/out should be totally decoupled from the core logic.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: new Strategy(options: { domain: string, clientID: string, clientSecret: string, callbackURL: string, state?: boolean, expectedIssuer?: string, authorizationURL?: string, tokenURL?: string, userInfoURL?: string, apiUrl?: string, maxAge?: number, [key: string]: any })

trigger_keywords: what endpoints are derived from the domain
which URLs get generated automatically
how are the protocol URLs constructed from the host
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Five endpoints are derived from the provider domain: expectedIssuer is https://<domain>/, authorizationURL is https://<domain>/authorize, tokenURL is https://<domain>/oauth/token, userInfoURL is https://<domain>/userinfo, and apiUrl is https://<domain>/api. All five can be individually overridden by passing explicit values in the config object.

trigger_keywords: immutability of caller options
does the constructor modify the input object
should the original config object be mutated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The constructor must NOT mutate the caller's original options object. The strategy must copy configuration onto a separate internal object. The caller's original object must never gain derived endpoint fields (like authorizationURL, tokenURL, etc.) as a side effect of construction. The test verifies both that the strategy's config is a distinct object and that the caller's object is free of derived fields.

trigger_keywords: default value for state flag
what is the default anti-forgery setting
is state enabled by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The anti-forgery state flag defaults to true when not explicitly provided. When the caller explicitly passes state: false or state: true, that value is preserved exactly as supplied.

trigger_keywords: how are auth params assembled
which login hint parameters are forwarded
what is the fixed order of authorization parameters
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The authorization parameters are output in this fixed order: connection, connection_scope, audience, prompt, login_hint, acr_values, max_age, nonce. Each is only included if it passes a type check (must be a string for text fields). connection_scope is only forwarded when connection is also present. max_age is only forwarded when numerically configured on the strategy itself (not per-request). nonce is only forwarded when previously recorded on the strategy's internal auth params (not per-request). If nothing is forwarded, output a single (none) line.

trigger_keywords: what is the token validation sequence
claim check order for ID token
which claims are validated and in what priority
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The ID token claim validation must follow this exact sequence: (1) token presence — error token_missing; (2) issuer present — error issuer_missing; (3) issuer match — error issuer_mismatch with expected= and actual= context; (4) subject present — error subject_missing; (5) algorithm allowed (RS256 or HS256 only) — error unsupported_algorithm with alg= context; (6) audience valid (string or array of strings) — error audience_invalid; (7) audience match — error audience_mismatch with expected= context; (8) authorized party when audience is multi-value: present — error azp_missing, matching expected audience — error azp_mismatch with expected= and actual=; (9) nonce if expected: present — error nonce_missing, matching — error nonce_mismatch with expected= and actual=; (10) exp is a number — error exp_invalid; (11) token not expired — error token_expired; (12) iat is a number — error iat_invalid; (13) if maxAge configured: auth_time present and numeric — error auth_time_invalid, not too old — error auth_time_expired.

trigger_keywords: how is the provider extracted from the user profile
pipe-delimited user ID handling
how does profile normalization work for different record formats
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Profile normalization handles three ID sources in priority order: user_id field (legacy format), sub field (OIDC format), or absent. The provider is derived from: (1) the first entry in the identities array if present, or (2) the prefix before the first '|' character in the user_id/sub if no identities array exists. If no '|' delimiter is present or no ID exists, provider is null/absent and rendered as (none). The normalized profile fields are: id, user_id (both from user_id or sub), displayName (from name), provider, givenName (from given_name), familyName (from family_name), emails (single email wrapped as a value-object list, or absent). The output order is: id, user_id, displayName, provider, givenName, familyName, emails.

trigger_keywords: how is the callback error handled
what happens when the provider returns an error in the callback
short circuit on provider error code
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the incoming callback request carries a provider error code (e.g., in the query string), authentication must abort immediately and surface that error code as the failure challenge. The adapter reports this as fail=<error_code>. This check must happen before any token exchange or validation attempt.

trigger_keywords: how is the token decoded
JWT decoding structure
what does the decode operation return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A compact token is three dot-separated base64url segments: header JSON, payload JSON, signature. Decoding parses the header and payload into structured claims, keeps the signature segment verbatim as the string 'sig', and retains the original raw token string for round-trip verification. The decode output reports: alg from header, then all payload claims in alphabetical key order, then signature=sig, then raw_matches=true.

trigger_keywords: custom headers on the strategy
how are caller-supplied headers preserved
custom request headers configuration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Caller-supplied custom request headers passed in the configuration are preserved on the constructed strategy and accessible. The adapter reports them as header.<headerName>=<headerValue> for each key in the customHeaders map.

trigger_keywords: code organization requirements
file structure for the library
which concerns should be in separate modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be split into distinct modules/files by concern: (1) strategy configuration and endpoint derivation, (2) login/authorization parameter assembly, (3) ID token decoding, (4) ID token claim validation, (5) user profile normalization, (6) the execution adapter (JSON stdin/stdout handling). The adapter must be fully decoupled from core logic — it only translates JSON commands into calls to core modules and renders results as key=value lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'config', 'immutability', 'custom_headers', 'auth_params', 'authenticate', 'decode', 'verify', 'profile'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the same claim-by-claim validation ordering we settled on during that login module refactor
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The exact sequential claim validation order defined in Feature 5 of the PRD: token_missing → issuer_missing → issuer_mismatch → subject_missing → unsupported_algorithm → audience_invalid → audience_mismatch → azp_missing/azp_mismatch → nonce_missing/nonce_mismatch → exp_invalid → token_expired → iat_invalid → auth_time_invalid → auth_time_expired

context_pointers.vague_reference: the 'pipe-delimited ID' edge cases the same way we discussed
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Profile normalization logic in Feature 6: when no identities array is present, split the user_id or sub on the first '|' character to extract the provider prefix; if no '|' exists, provider is null/absent

context_pointers.vague_reference: that config setup must not touch the caller's original options object
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1.4 (option immutability): the Strategy constructor must deep-copy or separately assign derived fields onto an internal options object, never writing authorizationURL, tokenURL, userInfoURL, expectedIssuer, or apiUrl back onto the object reference passed in by the caller

context_pointers.vague_reference: the adapter layer that handles the JSON in/out should be totally decoupled from the core logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter (the main entry point reading from stdin and writing to stdout) must only parse JSON commands and call into the core library modules; all validation, normalization, and parameter-assembly logic lives exclusively in the core modules with no I/O dependencies

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — please check the existing codebase and test cases for the expected behavior, and align with the overall pattern of the other similar features already implemented in the library.

feature1_1_default_endpoints.json.description: Constructing a strategy with only the mandatory credentials (an identity-provider host, a client identifier, a client secret, and a callback path) derives the full set of protocol endpoint URLs from the host and enables the anti-forgery state parameter by default. The adapter reports each derived endpoint and the default state flag.

feature1_2_explicit_overrides.json.description: When the caller supplies explicit endpoint URLs in addition to the host, the explicit values take precedence and are never clobbered by the host-derived defaults. The adapter reports the effective endpoints, which here are the caller-provided ones.

feature1_3_state_flag.json.description: The anti-forgery state flag is configurable. When the caller explicitly sets it, the supplied boolean value is preserved on the constructed strategy rather than being reset to the default.

feature1_4_option_immutability.json.description: Constructing a strategy must not mutate the caller's original options object. The strategy stores its effective configuration on a separate internal object, and the caller's object never gains the host-derived endpoint fields.

feature1_5_custom_headers.json.description: Caller-supplied custom request headers are preserved on the constructed strategy. The adapter reports the value of each caller-provided header key.

feature2_1_connection.json.description: The login-request parameter builder maps a textual connection selector into the outgoing authorization parameters. A dependent connection-scope selector is only forwarded when a connection selector is also present. Non-textual values for either selector are ignored. The adapter prints each forwarded parameter as key=value in a fixed order, or a placeholder line when nothing is forwarded.

feature2_2_audience.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards a textual API audience identifier into the outgoing authorization parameters, and ignores a non-textual audience value.

feature2_3_prompt.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards a textual prompt directive into the outgoing authorization parameters, and ignores a non-textual prompt value.

feature2_4_login_hint.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards a textual login-hint into the outgoing authorization parameters, and ignores a non-textual login-hint value.

feature2_5_acr_values.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards a textual authentication-context-class-reference selector into the outgoing authorization parameters, ignores a non-textual value, and forwards nothing when the selector is absent.

feature2_6_max_age.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards a maximum-authentication-age parameter only when a numeric maximum age is configured on the strategy itself; the value supplied per-request is irrelevant. A non-numeric configured value, or an unconfigured value, forwards nothing for this parameter.

feature2_7_nonce.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards a textual nonce into the outgoing authorization parameters only when a nonce was recorded on the strategy during an earlier login step. A per-request nonce value is never consulted, and nothing is forwarded when no nonce was recorded.

feature2_8_empty.json.description: The login-request parameter builder forwards no parameters when given an empty options object, and treats an absent options argument identically to an empty one.

feature3_authenticate_error.json.description: When an incoming callback request carries a provider error code in its query string, authentication is aborted and that error code is propagated unchanged as the failure challenge instead of attempting to exchange an authorization code. The adapter reports the captured failure challenge.

feature4_decode.json.description: A compact token is the concatenation of three dot-separated segments: a base64-encoded JSON header, a base64-encoded JSON payload, and a signature segment. Decoding parses the header and payload back into structured claims, keeps the signature segment verbatim, and retains the original raw token string. The adapter reports the header algorithm, every decoded payload claim, the signature segment, and whether the raw token round-tripped.

cases.expected_output:
alg=RS256
aud=sample-client
exp=9999999999
iat=1000000000
iss=https://login.example.com/
sub=user|123
signature=sig
raw_matches=true

alg=HS256
iss=https://login.example.com/
name=ÁÁutf8
sub=user|123
signature=sig
raw_matches=true

feature5_1_token_presence.json.description: Verification rejects a request that carries no token at all, reporting a neutral missing-token category before any claim inspection.

feature5_2_issuer.json.description: Verification requires the issuer claim to be a present string and to equal the expected issuer. A missing issuer yields a neutral issuer-missing category; a present but non-matching issuer yields an issuer-mismatch category carrying the expected and actual issuer values as separate fields.

feature5_3_subject.json.description: Verification requires the subject claim to be a present string. A missing subject yields a neutral subject-missing category.

feature5_4_algorithm.json.description: Verification only accepts tokens signed with one of two approved signature algorithms. A token whose header declares any other algorithm is rejected with a neutral unsupported-algorithm category that carries the offending algorithm name as a separate field.

feature5_5_audience.json.description: Verification requires the audience claim to be a present string or array of strings, and the expected audience must be the audience (string form) or be contained in it (array form). A missing/invalid audience yields an audience-invalid category; an expected audience absent from the claim yields an audience-mismatch category carrying the expected audience as a separate field.

feature5_6_authorized_party.json.description: When the audience claim contains multiple values, verification requires an authorized-party claim that is a present string equal to the expected audience. A missing authorized party yields an azp-missing category; a non-matching authorized party yields an azp-mismatch category carrying the expected and actual values as separate fields.

feature5_7_nonce.json.description: When an expected nonce is supplied, verification requires the nonce claim to be a present string equal to it. A missing nonce yields a nonce-missing category; a non-matching nonce yields a nonce-mismatch category carrying the expected and actual nonce values as separate fields.

feature5_8_expiration.json.description: Verification requires the expiration claim to be a number and the token must not have expired (with a small clock-skew allowance), and requires the issued-at claim to be a present number. A non-numeric expiration yields an exp-invalid category; an expiration already in the past yields a token-expired category; a missing issued-at yields an iat-invalid category.

feature5_9_max_age.json.description: When a maximum authentication age is configured, verification requires an authentication-time claim that is a present number and that the elapsed time since authentication does not exceed the maximum (with a small clock-skew allowance). A missing authentication time yields an auth-time-invalid category; an authentication that is too old yields an auth-time-expired category.

feature6_1_with_identities.json.description: A raw user record that includes an identities list is normalized into a standard profile: the display name comes from the record's name, the identifier comes from the record's user id, the provider is taken from the first identity entry, the given/family names are mapped into a structured name, and a single email is wrapped into a list of value objects. The adapter reports each normalized field.

cases.expected_output:
id=samlp|u-1
user_id=samlp|u-1
displayName=Jane Doe
provider=samlp
givenName=Jane
familyName=Doe
emails=user@example.com

feature6_2_provider_from_id.json.description: When a raw user record has no identities list but its identifier contains a provider-qualified prefix delimited by a vertical bar, the provider is derived from that prefix. All other standard fields are normalized as usual.

cases.expected_output:
id=samlp|u-1
user_id=samlp|u-1
displayName=Jane Doe
provider=samlp
givenName=Jane
familyName=Doe
emails=user@example.com

feature6_3_oidc_sub.json.description: An OpenID-Connect style raw record carries its identifier under a subject field rather than a user-id field. Normalization uses the subject as the identifier while still deriving the provider from the first identity entry and mapping all other standard fields.

cases.expected_output:
id=samlp|u-1
user_id=samlp|u-1
displayName=Jane Doe
provider=samlp
givenName=Jane
familyName=Doe
emails=user@example.com

feature6_4_no_id.json.description: A raw record with neither an identities list nor any identifier produces a profile that exposes no provider field at all.

cases.expected_output:
id=(none)
user_id=(none)
displayName=(none)
provider=(none)
givenName=(none)
familyName=(none)
emails=user@example.com

feature6_5_id_without_provider.json.description: A raw record whose identifier contains no provider-qualifying delimiter yields a profile that exposes the identifier verbatim but exposes no provider field.

cases.expected_output:
id=plain-subject-id
user_id=plain-subject-id
displayName=(none)
provider=(none)
givenName=(none)
familyName=(none)
emails=(none)

feature6_6_no_email.json.description: A raw record that carries no email produces a profile that exposes no email collection.

cases.expected_output:
id=plain-subject-id
user_id=plain-subject-id
displayName=(none)
provider=(none)
givenName=(none)
familyName=(none)
emails=(none)
